data.title: AMETEK Director Trades $114K In Company Stock
data.description:
Revealing a significant insider sell on March 25, THOMAS AMATO, Director at AMETEK (NYSE:AME), as per the latest SEC filing.

What Happened: AMATO's recent Form 4 filing with the U.S. Securities and Exchange Commission on Wednesday unveiled the sale of 530 shares of AMETEK. The total transaction value is $114,893.

The latest update on Thursday morning shows AMETEK shares down by 0.47%, trading at $214.31.

Unveiling the Story Behind AMETEK

Breaking Down AMETEK's Financial Performance

Positive Revenue Trend: Examining AMETEK's financials over 3 months reveals a positive narrative. The company achieved a noteworthy revenue growth rate of 13.44% as of 31 December, 2025, showcasing a substantial increase in top-line earnings. When compared to others in the Industrials sector, the company faces challenges, achieving a growth rate lower than the average among peers.

Interpreting Earnings Metrics:

Debt Management: The company maintains a balanced debt approach with a debt-to-equity ratio below industry norms, standing at 0.22.

Market Valuation:

Market Capitalization: Surpassing industry standards, the company's market capitalization asserts its dominance in terms of size, suggesting a robust market position.

The Importance of Insider Transactions

Insider transactions contribute to decision-making but should be supplemented by a comprehensive investment analysis.

When discussing legal matters, the term "insider" refers to any officer, director, or beneficial owner holding more than ten percent of a company's equity securities, as stipulated in Section 12 of the Securities Exchange Act of 1934. This includes executives in the c-suite and significant hedge funds. Such insiders are required to report their transactions through a Form 4 filing, which must be completed within two business days of the transaction.

A new purchase by a company insider is a indication that they anticipate the stock will rise.

On the other hand, insider sells may not necessarily indicate a bearish view and can be motivated by various factors.

data.title: CEO Of iRadimed Sold $752K In Stock
data.description:
Making a noteworthy insider sell on March 25, Roger E Susi, CEO at iRadimed (NASDAQ:IRMD), is reported in the latest SEC filing.

What Happened: A Form 4 filing with the U.S. Securities and Exchange Commission on Wednesday outlined that Susi executed a sale of 7,500 shares of iRadimed with a total value of $752,609.

iRadimed's shares are actively trading at $101.78, experiencing a down of 0.62% during Thursday's morning session.

Get to Know iRadimed Better

Financial Milestones: iRadimed's Journey

Revenue Growth: Over the 3 months period, iRadimed showcased positive performance, achieving a revenue growth rate of 17.03% as of 31 December, 2025. This reflects a substantial increase in the company's top-line earnings. When compared to others in the Health Care sector, the company excelled with a growth rate higher than the average among peers.

Profitability Metrics:

  • Gross Margin: With a high gross margin of 75.06%, the company demonstrates effective cost control and strong profitability relative to its peers.

  • Earnings per Share (EPS): iRadimed's EPS lags behind the industry average, indicating concerns and potential challenges with a current EPS of 0.51.

Debt Management: iRadimed's debt-to-equity ratio is below industry norms, indicating a sound financial structure with a ratio of 0.0.

Valuation Metrics: A Closer Look

Market Capitalization Perspectives: The company's market capitalization falls below industry averages, signaling a relatively smaller size compared to peers. This positioning may be influenced by factors such as perceived growth potential or operational scale.

Why Pay Attention to Insider Transactions

Insider transactions shouldn't be used primarily to make an investing decision, however an insider transaction can be an important factor in the investing decision.

Within the legal framework, an "insider" is defined as any officer, director, or beneficial owner holding more than ten percent of a company's equity securities as per Section 12 of the Securities Exchange Act of 1934. This includes executives in the c-suite and major hedge funds. These insiders are mandated to disclose their transactions through a Form 4 filing, to be submitted within two business days of the transaction.

The initiation of a new purchase by a company insider serves as a strong indication that they expect the stock to rise.

However, insider sells may not always signal a bearish view and can be influenced by various factors.

data.title: Millennials Feel 80% Confident About Their Finances — Until Their Situation Gets Complicated. Then It Drops To 27%
data.description:
Joke all you want about their penchant for six-figure student loans and $18 avocado toast, for many millennials the strategy for confronting financial chaos is no strategy at all. 

Are they confident? Yes. Doing well? Not exactly, as just 27% of millennials feel confident handling a diverse portfolio of assets, according to a survey issued by Equitable. 

Millennials' Unboxed Chaos 

Millennials have so many reasons to want to look away and try to sound brave. It's not just because, as the preliminary University of Michigan Consumer Sentiment Survey revealed, most consumers are feeling anxious about spending and where the economy will end up. 

That general anxiety becomes quite specific when you drill down to millennials’ key concerns. Not only have 3 out of 5 millennials and Gen Zers reported draining their bank accounts and hitting overdraft in 2025, according to a US News survey, but 38% have also recently missed credit card payments. 

According to a recent survey by Intuit, 61% of adults see money as their primary life stressor and 54% state they have financial regrets from 2025 that are impacting them right now. Other bad things millennials have besides stress and regret? Debt—lots of it. 

They have the highest average student loan amounts ($40,238) next to Gen X, ($44, 240) and the largest share of student debt. Millennials also get to be best with how much they owe on their mortgages. They have the highest average mortgage balances at $320,027. 

That means millennials–facing a future squeeze from aging parents potentially requiring long-term care and college-bound kids—want to accumulate wealth, fast. 

While some millennials are simply moving from higher-earner hubs like New York and San Francisco to cities where costs are lower, others are taking a more Monte Carlo approach and throwing pretty much anything against the wall.  

When Dollars and Cents Don't Make Sense 

New research shows that many Americans, attempting to meet uncertainty with confidence, are leaning in on prediction markets, crypto, and credit cards to create a Frankenstein financial security plan—often without professional guidance.  

That effort to get richer quicker highlights the complex relationship millennials have with financial insight. While many millennials report they prefer a hybrid approach to financial education and planning—a blend of self-help tools and personalized advising-–many  are still using a shotgun approach to financial management. 

That means no research, no due diligence and a lot of TikTok. 

SmartAsset’s matching platform is designed to make finding that kind of advisor straightforward—one short questionnaire, up to three matches, and a side-by-side comparison of advisors who work with clients at exactly this stage of financial complexity.

For those who don't, the triage can be messy. 

According to the Northwestern Mutual study, 62% of millennials admit that they "suffer from a common blind spot: they place too much emphasis on building wealth/growing their assets without dedicating enough to protecting their assets and managing against risks."  

The consequences of flying blind during times of economic uncertainty can be disastrous—especially when there's no one to turn to for strategy advice.

For millennials, burdened with debt and dealing with rising costs, an anything-goes approach means seeing what works and getting advice from anywhere—even random posters on TikTok.  

Transforming Complexity into Cash 

While those millennials and GenZ love RichTok, most know that social media offers guesses, not real guidance. 

That gets easier when advisors work with simple-to-use digital tools that allow clients to feel in control of their finances at any moment of the day—a key selling point for digital-native millennials who want guidance but are not about to sit through a three-hour meeting at a bank branch. 

Image: Shutterstock

data.title: 'Ramsey Show' Hosts React To A Husband Going On Cruises Without His Wife. 'I Think He's Cheating On You. Guys Don't Do R&R On Their Own'
data.description:
A husband taking solo cruises without his wife while hiding major financial decisions, raised serious concerns about what might be happening behind the scenes in one marriage.

Financial Secrecy And Solo Trips Raise Red Flags

Amy explained that her husband had recently bought a vehicle without discussing it and regularly booked vacations on his own. At the time of the call, he was already on a solo cruise, with two more planned, also without her. 

Don't Miss:

“That's not a thing, Amy. I think he's cheating on you,” Kamel responded to that information. “Guys don't do R&R on their own to get away on a trip.”

He added that the refusal to combine finances and the secretive behavior suggested something deeper.

“I think the parts you know are the tip of the iceberg,” Kamel said.

“When you financially hide money, there's usually another person involved. There's an addiction involved. There are not good things,” Cruze said. “You don't hide money because you're really smart and kind.”

Trending: Think you're saving enough for your kids? You might be dangerously off — see why

A Marriage In Name Only

As the conversation continued, Cruze said she didn't believe the relationship functioned as a real partnership.

“I don't think you have a marriage, Amy,” she said. “You do not have a godly husband who is loving and serving you well.”

The situation became even more concerning when Amy revealed that her husband didn't want her to join one of the upcoming cruises. “He was like, ‘Well, I don’t know how to tell you this, but I don’t want you to go,'” she recalled.

“He doesn't want to be married to you,” Kamel said, pointing to the pattern of secrecy, independent decisions and lack of shared life.

“I would be convinced that there's somebody else,” she said.

Protecting Yourself Financially

While neither host explicitly told Amy to leave, both said that staying would mean accepting the current reality.

“You have to decide the life that you want for you,” Cruze said.

“The best thing you can do is just start to kind of protect yourself and create your own little island so that you're okay financially,” Kamel added.

The conversation pointed to something the show talks about a lot: money problems are usually tied to bigger issues in the relationship. In this case, the lack of shared accounts, hidden spending and unexplained trips pointed to a breakdown in trust that goes far beyond budgeting.

Image: Shutterstock
